A Judas figure or papier-mâché skeleton rests above on the canopy frame.\n\nThe iconography here is extraordinarily specific and art-historically significant. The skeletal figure above references the Judas figures common in Mexican folk tradition, the medical apparatus alludes to Kahlo's lifelong physical suffering from her bus accident injuries, and the vines growing around the sleeping figure (self-portrait) reference themes of death, life, and entanglement that pervade her work.\n\nFrida Kahlo's market has experienced extraordinary appreciation over the past two decades. Her works are among the most valuable by any Latin American artist. Key auction records include 'Two Nudes in a Forest' selling for approximately $8 million, and 'Roots' (Raíces) selling for $5.6 million in 2006. More recently, 'Diego y yo' sold at Sotheby's in November 2021 for $34.9 million, setting a new record for a Latin American artwork at auction. Her major works now regularly command eight-figure sums.\n\n'The Dream' is a major, large-format work from 1940 — a pivotal year in Kahlo's career (the same year she painted 'The Two Fridas'). The work is currently held in a private collection (the Selma and Nesuhi Ertegun Collection, as far as public records indicate), meaning it has not been on the open market in recent decades.\n\nCondition, as visible in this reproduction, appears generally good, though the image quality prevents detailed assessment of craquelure, surface texture, or restoration work.\n\nGiven the current Kahlo market, the historical significance of this particular work (1940, major thematic painting, large format), and comparables including the 2021 'Diego y yo' record, a work of this caliber and importance would conservatively estimate in the range of $25–50 million at auction today, with strong potential to exceed that given the right bidding environment and buyer interest from Latin American collectors, major museums, and international private collectors. parsed.internal_reasoning:
This painting presents a highly distinctive and immediately recognizable surrealist composition depicting a figure sleeping in a four-poster bed floating among clouds, with a skeletal or mannequin-like figure lying on a canopy above the bed, surrounded by what appear to be medical/orthopedic devices, wire, and botanical elements. The sleeping figure below is covered by a golden-orange blanket with vine/leaf motifs, and vines appear to grow from or around the figure. A Judas figure or papier-mâché skeleton rests above on the canopy frame.

The iconography here is extraordinarily specific and art-historically significant. This is almost certainly 'The Dream (El Sueño)' by Frida Kahlo, painted in 1940. The work is oil on canvas, approximately 74 × 98.5 cm. The skeletal figure above references the Judas figures common in Mexican folk tradition, the medical apparatus alludes to Kahlo's lifelong physical suffering from her bus accident injuries, and the vines growing around the sleeping figure (self-portrait) reference themes of death, life, and entanglement that pervade her work.

Frida Kahlo's market has experienced extraordinary appreciation over the past two decades. Her works are among the most valuable by any Latin American artist. Key auction records include 'Two Nudes in a Forest' selling for approximately $8 million, and 'Roots' (Raíces) selling for $5.6 million in 2006. More recently, 'Diego y yo' sold at Sotheby's in November 2021 for $34.9 million, setting a new record for a Latin American artwork at auction. Her major works now regularly command eight-figure sums.

'The Dream' is a major, large-format work from 1940 — a pivotal year in Kahlo's career (the same year she painted 'The Two Fridas'). It is a deeply personal and iconographically rich painting with strong surrealist credentials. The work is currently held in a private collection (the Selma and Nesuhi Ertegun Collection, as far as public records indicate), meaning it has not been on the open market in recent decades.

Condition, as visible in this reproduction, appears generally good, though the image quality prevents detailed assessment of craquelure, surface texture, or restoration work.

Given the current Kahlo market, the historical significance of this particular work (1940, major thematic painting, large format), and comparables including the 2021 'Diego y yo' record, a work of this caliber and importance would conservatively estimate in the range of $25–50 million at auction today, with strong potential to exceed that given the right bidding environment and buyer interest from Latin American collectors, major museums, and international private collectors. I would estimate fair market value at approximately $35 million, reflecting its status as a major but not the absolute pinnacle Kahlo work.
key_factors: Attributed to Frida Kahlo — one of the most commercially valuable artists of the 20th century with record-breaking recent auction results
Major thematic work from 1940, a pivotal year in Kahlo's career coinciding with her most celebrated period
Rich surrealist iconography incorporating Mexican folk tradition (Judas figure), medical trauma symbolism, and autobiographical narrative
Large-format oil on canvas with strong art-historical and cultural significance to Latin American modernism
Comparable sales including 'Diego y yo' ($34.9M, 2021) establish benchmark for top-tier Kahlo works at auction
